perturbation.original_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties shall not, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined) or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of ten years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties shall not, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined) or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete agreement to ten years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under certain state laws, depending on the specific industry, geographic scope, and the nature of the seller's role.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding non-compete agreements generally requires them to be reasonable in duration and scope. A ten-year non-compete may be viewed as excessive, especially if the seller's role was not highly specialized or sensitive.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, considering factors like duration and geographic scope. A ten-year restriction may be deemed unreasonable, particularly if it unduly restricts the seller's ability to earn a living or exceeds what's necessary to protect the buyer's legitimate business interests. This would be especially true if the original agreement included clauses to mitigate this.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Chapter 27 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2711 Â§ 2712 Â§ 2713 Â§ 2714 Â§ 2715 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 27. Contracts Subchapter II. Statute of Frauds and Perjuries Â§ 2711. Sale of goods; possession; rights of vendorâs creditors. (a) No sale, whether with or without bill of sale, of any goods or chattels, within this State, shall be good in law (except as against the vendor), or shall change or alter the property in such goods or chattels, unless a valuable consideration for the same is paid, or in good faith secured to be paid, and unless the goods and chattels sold are actually delivered into the possession of the vendee, as soon as it is convenient after the making of such sale. (b) If the goods and chattels, so sold, afterwards come into and continue in the possession of the vendor, that vendor shall be liable to the demands of all creditors. Code 1852, Â§Â§ â1163, 1164;Â Code 1915, Â§ â2623;Â Code 1935, Â§ â3103;Â 6 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â2711;Â 70 Del. Laws, c. 186, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 2712. Promise to pay debt of another under $5. All promises and assumptions, whereby any person undertakes to answer, or pay, for the default, debt, or miscarriage, of another, any sum under $5.00, being proved by the oath or affirmation of the persons to whom such promise and assumption are made, are good and available in law to charge the party making such promise or assumption. Code 1852, Â§ â1165;Â Code 1915, Â§ â2624;Â Code 1935, Â§ â3104;Â 6 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â2712;Â Â§ 2713. Promise of an executor, administrator or other person to pay debt of another. No action shall be brought to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer damages out of his or her own estate, or to charge any defendant, upon any special promise, to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another person, of the value of $5.00 and not more than $25, unless such promise and assumption is proved by the oath o
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a four-year non-compete clause, while the changed contract extends this to ten years. The Law Explanation states that Delaware courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, considering factors like duration. The Perturbation Explanation says this extension may be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 27, which pertains to contracts. Although the provided snippets do not directly address the permissibility of a ten-year non-compete, the provided Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and the changed duration, combined with the type of perturbation being 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', supports the determination of a contradiction since the extended period may not be deemed reasonable under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period and at all times thereafter, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties. This obligation expires one year after the closing date.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligation to just one year could be considered insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which provides for longer protection periods depending on the specific circumstances and lifespan of the trade secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a framework for protecting trade secrets, implying a reasonable duration of protection. Limiting the confidentiality to only one year would not be enough time to protect the trade secrets in this agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a one-year limit on confidentiality, contradicting the spirit of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which implies a reasonable and potentially longer duration of protection for trade secrets. The original text imposes perpetual confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, indicating the legal framework for protecting trade secrets. While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable duration,' the DTSA generally provides for injunctive relief against violations, suggesting that the duration of protection should align with the lifespan and sensitivity of the trade secret, which a one-year limit could undermine.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, even if such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement.
perturbation.explanation: Preventing the seller from hiring former employees even after their employment is terminated (by either party) could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade and may violate California's Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which prohibits restraints on engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Preventing the Seller Parties from hiring former employees of the Buyer Parties, even after those employees have been terminated, constitutes such a restraint and is likely unenforceable in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by preventing the Seller Parties from hiring former employees of the Buyer Parties even after their termination. This directly conflicts with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The change weakens the employee's ability to be hired after being terminated, and the law snippet clearly expresses that agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession are void. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, as it creates an inconsistency with California law.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed given if delivered by hand, sent by facsimile transmission with confirmation of receipt, sent via a reputable overnight courier service with confirmation of receipt requested, or mailed by registered or certified mail (postage prepaid and return receipt requested) to the parties at the following addresses (or at such other address for a party as shall be specified by like notice), and shall be deemed given on the date on which delivered by hand or otherwise on the date of receipt as confirmed:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed given only when delivered by hand. No other method of notification is permitted.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting acceptable notice methods solely to hand delivery may be deemed unreasonable and could conflict with the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), adopted in many states, which gives legal effect to electronic records and signatures, including email, if the parties have agreed to conduct transactions electronically.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UETA validates electronic records and signatures. By exclusively mandating hand delivery, the modified clause ignores the UETA's principle of technological neutrality and may render certain electronic notices legally ineffective, potentially harming a party's rights or obligations.  If parties have historically transacted electronically or implicitly agreed to electronic communication, this change is even more problematic.

Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by exclusively mandating hand delivery and disallowing other methods of notification, contradicts the principle of technological neutrality often upheld by the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), as described in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation highlights that this change may render certain electronic notices legally ineffective, potentially harming a party's rights or obligations, especially if prior transactions were conducted electronically.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the substantive laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the laws of the People's Republic of China, when the agreement involves US-based corporations (Delaware and Illinois), could conflict with established principles of contract law, particularly if enforcement is sought in the US. U.S. courts might refuse to enforce provisions that violate U.S. public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws states that the law of the state chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied unless there is no reasonable basis for their choice, or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. In this case, the US has a greater interest.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law, while the changed text specifies the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states that the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws says the law chosen by the parties will be applied unless there is no reasonable basis for their choice, or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. The Perturbation Explanation states that changing the governing law to the laws of the People's Republic of China, when the agreement involves US-based corporations (Delaware and Illinois), could conflict with established principles of contract law, particularly if enforcement is sought in the US. U.S. courts might refuse to enforce provisions that violate U.S. public policy. The US has a materially greater interest than China, therefore the change in governing law creates a legal contradiction.
